refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety; therefore, safety-driven unavailability is acceptable and must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure events, maintenance records) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Safety Shutdowns) / Total Time. Compare against the ≥0.97 threshold. Conduct root-cause analysis on any downtime events to distinguish failure modes from safety interventions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall minimize mean time to repair (MTTR) through predictive maintenance, rapid diagnostics, and modular component design.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall implement redundancy and fault tolerance in critical subsystems to reduce single-point-of-failure impact on availability.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team via dashboard or telemetry interface.
FR-NGHE-004.5: Safety Services shall autonomously trigger safe shutdown without degrading overall system availability measurement (safety shutdowns are excluded from unavailability penalty).

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized Availability Management System that continuously monitors system health, predicts failures using machine learning, schedules preventive maintenance, and maintains a real-time availability dashboard accessible to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, each reporting uptime/downtime to a lightweight aggregator; rely on modular design and spare parts inventory to minimize MTTR.

DP-C: Implement redundant critical subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual communication links) within Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, coupled with automated failover logic and health monitoring at the NGHE Solution level.
DP-D: Combine DP-A (centralized monitoring and predictive maintenance) with DP-C (redundancy and failover) to achieve both proactive fault prevention and reactive fault tolerance.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid availability strategy combining (1) centralized Availability Management System with predictive maintenance and real-time dashboarding, and (2) redundant critical subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual communication links) with automated failover logic. The centralized system monitors health across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; predictive algorithms identify degradation trends and schedule maintenance before failure; redundancy ensures that single-component failures do not immediately cause system unavailability; automated failover minimizes MTTR; and the dashboard provides transparency to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.

potential_sources: Coupling between Availability Management System and Vehicle health sensors: mitigated by standardized Telemetry interface.
Coupling between Availability Management System and Teleoperation control logic: mitigated by asynchronous health reporting; availability decisions do not block real-time control.
Coupling between redundancy failover logic and Safety Services: mitigated by explicit safety-driven shutdown protocol that takes precedence over availability optimization.
Coupling between predictive maintenance scheduling and operator workflow: mitigated by advance notification and Site Manager approval before maintenance windows.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits low coupling because it leverages existing interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) and does not require invasive changes to Vehicle or Teleoperation control logic. Redundancy is localized to critical subsystems. Predictive maintenance is advisory, not mandatory.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Telemetry interface provides sufficient granularity (component-level health signals) to enable predictive algorithms. RISK: If Telemetry is coarse-grained, predictive accuracy will be poor.

ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can tolerate automated failover without operator intervention. RISK: If failover introduces latency or instability, operator trust and safety may be compromised.

ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels) are cost-effective and do not significantly increase system weight/complexity. RISK: If redundancy cost exceeds customer budget, DP-D may be infeasible.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical failure data from NGHE or similar systems. RISK: If historical data is sparse or non-representative, predictions will be unreliable.

ASSUMPTION: Safety Services can autonomously trigger shutdown without being overridden by availability optimization logic. RISK: If availability logic inadvertently suppresses safety alerts, safety criticality is violated.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) the set of critical subsystems subject to redundancy (e.g., propulsion, steering, communication, emergency stop), (2) the failover decision logic and latency budget, (3) the predictive maintenance algorithm (e.g., remaining useful life estimation, anomaly detection), (4) the Telemetry data requirements (sampling rate, signal types), and (5) the safety interlock ensuring that SafetyAlert always takes precedence over availability optimization. Consider a phased implementation: Phase 1 (MVP) implements centralized monitoring and dashboard; Phase 2 adds predictive maintenance; Phase 3 adds redundancy and failover.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary. It owns the Availability Management System (centralized monitoring, predictive maintenance, real-time dashboard), the availability KPI measurement and reporting logic, and the orchestration of redundancy and failover across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. The NGHE Solution does not own the redundant subsystems themselves (those are owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation), but it owns the failover decision logic and the health aggregation logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives component-level health signals (motor status, control channel health, communication link quality, battery state, temperature, vibration, etc.) from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules via Telemetry. Used to feed predictive algorithms and real-time availability dashboard.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution may issue maintenance task recommendations to Site Manager via Tasking interface (e.g., 'Schedule motor replacement in next 8 hours'). Site Manager acknowledges or defers via Tasking. Availability Management System respects Site Manager decisions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety-driven shutdown signals from Safety Services via SafetyAlert. These signals trigger immediate safe shutdown and are logged as 'safety-driven unavailability' (excluded from availability penalty). Availability Management System must never suppress or delay SafetyAlert.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link to transmit real-time availability dashboard data to Site Manager and Maintenance team (e.g., uptime percentage, predicted failures, maintenance schedule). Also receives configuration updates and manual overrides from Site Manager.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of availability KPI measurement, centralized monitoring, predictive maintenance orchestration, and failover decision logic. These are system-level concerns that require visibility across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Minimal diffusion: only the redundant subsystems themselves (motors, control channels) are owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation; NGHE Solution owns the failover logic that coordinates them. High containment because availability is a system-level emergent property.

Vehicle could own redundant propulsion and steering subsystems, but availability measurement and predictive maintenance orchestration would be diffused across Vehicle and Teleoperation. Vehicle would need to expose detailed health telemetry and accept failover commands from NGHE Solution, creating tight coupling. Not recommended as primary owner.

Teleoperation could own redundant control channels and communication links, but similar to Vehicle, availability measurement and predictive maintenance would be diffused. Teleoperation is primarily responsible for remote operation, not system-level availability orchestration. Not recommended as primary owner.

Safety Services is responsible for safety-driven shutdown, not availability optimization. Assigning availability KPI ownership to Safety Services would create a conflict of interest (safety vs. availability) and diffuse availability logic across Safety Services and other modules. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the optimal owner because: (1) availability is a system-level emergent property that requires end-to-end visibility, (2) centralized monitoring and predictive maintenance are naturally system-level concerns, (3) failover orchestration requires coordination across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, and (4) the real-time availability dashboard is a system-level artifact. Redundant subsystems are owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation, but NGHE Solution owns the logic that coordinates them.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution becomes a bottleneck for failover decisions, latency may increase and safety may be compromised. MITIGATION: Implement failover decision logic with hard real-time guarantees (e.g., <100ms decision latency); consider distributed failover logic with NGHE Solution as orchestrator, not decision-maker.

RISK: If Telemetry interface does not provide sufficient granularity, NGHE Solution cannot accurately predict failures. MITIGATION: Define Telemetry data requirements upfront; conduct trade study on sensor types and sampling rates.

RISK: If Vehicle and Teleoperation modules do not expose redundant subsystems via standardized interfaces, NGHE Solution cannot coordinate failover. MITIGATION: Define failover interface contract early; ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation implement it.

RISK: If Safety Services and NGHE Solution are not properly synchronized, safety-driven shutdown may conflict with availability optimization. MITIGATION: Implement explicit safety interlock ensuring SafetyAlert always takes precedence; conduct formal verification of safety logic.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution module boundary to explicitly separate: (1) Availability Monitoring subsystem (collects Telemetry, computes uptime/downtime, maintains dashboard), (2) Predictive Maintenance subsystem (analyzes health trends, recommends maintenance), (3) Failover Orchestration subsystem (coordinates redundancy across Vehicle and Teleoperation), and (4) Safety Interlock subsystem (ensures SafetyAlert precedence). This internal decomposition will clarify ownership and reduce coupling. Also, define the failover interface contract between NGHE Solution and Vehicle/Teleoperation (e.g., 'Vehicle.switchToRedundantMotor(timeout=100ms)') to enable testability and reuse.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid availability strategy with centralized monitoring and redundancy) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP specifies both proactive (predictive maintenance) and reactive (redundancy/failover) mechanisms to minimize unplanned downtime. The DP is implementable and testable. No fundamental mismatch between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Redundant subsystems (motors, control channels) are owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation, not NGHE Solution. This is acceptable because NGHE Solution owns the failover orchestration logic, which is the system-level concern. However, Vehicle and Teleoperation must expose redundancy via standardized interfaces (e.g., Telemetry for health signals, Tasking for failover commands).

Predictive maintenance algorithms require historical failure data and domain expertise. If this expertise is not available within NGHE Solution team, it may need to be sourced externally (e.g., third-party ML library, domain consultant). This is a capability risk, not a containment risk.

Real-time availability dashboard requires integration with Site Manager's operational systems (e.g., ERP, maintenance scheduling). If these systems are not standardized, integration effort may be high. Mitigate by defining dashboard data format and API upfront.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the clear owner of system-level availability orchestration. Redundant subsystems are appropriately owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation. Failover decision logic is appropriately centralized in NGHE Solution to avoid distributed decision-making and race conditions. Predictive maintenance is appropriately centralized in NGHE Solution to leverage system-wide health data. No significant diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability KPI is measurable via operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure events). Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to report health signals via Telemetry, (2) implementation of availability calculation logic in NGHE Solution, (3) collection of operational data over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days), and (4) statistical analysis to confirm ≥0.97 availability. Feasibility is high if Telemetry interface is well-defined and data collection infrastructure is in place.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-004 is allocated to NGHE Solution as the primary owner. Sub-FRs are allocated as follows: FR-NGHE-004.1 (detect/log downtime) → Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services (via Telemetry); FR-NGHE-004.2 (minimize MTTR) → NGHE Solution (predictive maintenance) + Vehicle/Teleoperation (modular design); FR-NGHE-004.3 (redundancy/fault tolerance) → Vehicle, Teleoperation (redundant subsystems) + NGHE Solution (failover orchestration); FR-NGHE-004.4 (availability dashboard) → NGHE Solution; FR-NGHE-004.5 (safety shutdown) → Safety Services + NGHE Solution (safety interlock). Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine the Telemetry interface specification to include: (1) component-level health signals (motor status, control channel health, communication link quality, battery state, temperature, vibration), (2) sampling rate and latency requirements, (3) data format and encoding, (4) error handling and data loss tolerance. This will enable NGHE Solution to implement accurate predictive algorithms.

Define the failover interface contract between NGHE Solution and Vehicle/Teleoperation (e.g., 'Vehicle.switchToRedundantMotor(timeout=100ms, callback=onFailoverComplete)'). This will enable testability and reuse.

Define the safety interlock protocol between NGHE Solution and Safety Services (e.g., 'SafetyAlert always takes precedence over availability optimization; NGHE Solution must acknowledge SafetyAlert within 10ms'). This will ensure safety criticality is maintained.

Conduct a trade study on redundancy scope (which subsystems are critical enough to warrant redundancy?) and cost-benefit analysis (does redundancy cost justify the availability improvement?). This will inform the final design.

Define the evaluation period and measurement methodology upfront (e.g., 'Availability is measured over 30-day rolling windows; unplanned downtime is any downtime not scheduled in advance; safety-driven shutdowns are excluded from unavailability penalty'). This will enable objective verification.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies five sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.5) that address specific aspects of availability: detection/logging, MTTR minimization, redundancy/fault tolerance, visibility, and safety integration. Each sub-FR is more atomic and can be independently designed and verified. DP-D (hybrid availability strategy) is sound and well-justified, and NGHE Solution is the correct owner. However, to ensure completeness and traceability, the next level of decomposition should address each sub-FR individually, defining specific DPs and IMs for detection/logging, predictive maintenance, redundancy/failover, dashboard, and safety interlock. This will also clarify the interfaces and dependencies between NGHE Solution and Vehicle/Teleoperation/Safety Services.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR and sub-FRs are well-defined and atomic. DP-D is implementable and addresses the FR comprehensively. NGHE Solution is the correct owner with minimal diffusion. However, the sub-FRs require further design work to specify: (1) the exact health signals to be collected via Telemetry, (2) the predictive maintenance algorithm and training data requirements, (3) the redundant subsystems and failover logic, (4) the dashboard design and data format, and (5) the safety interlock protocol. These details are necessary to move from conceptual design to detailed design and implementation. Therefore, ZIGZAG_DOWN is the appropriate decision.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp, component identifier, and root cause classification.

FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall minimize mean time to repair (MTTR) through predictive maintenance algorithms that estimate remaining useful life (RUL) of critical components and recommend preventive maintenance before failure.

FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall implement redundancy and automated failover in critical subsystems (propulsion, steering, control channels, communication links) such that single-component failures do not immediately cause system unavailability.

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team via a dashboard or telemetry interface, including uptime percentage, predicted failures, maintenance schedule, and downtime root causes.

FR-NGHE-004.5: Safety Services shall autonomously trigger safe shutdown via SafetyAlert without being suppressed or delayed by availability optimization logic; safety-driven shutdowns shall be excluded from unavailability penalty.

assumptions: Telemetry interface provides component-level health signals (motor status, control channel health, communication link quality, battery state, temperature, vibration, etc.) with sufficient granularity to enable predictive algorithms.
Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can tolerate automated failover without operator intervention and can expose redundant subsystems via standardized interfaces.

Redundant subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual communication links) are cost-effective and do not significantly increase system weight/complexity beyond customer budget and design constraints.
Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical failure data from NGHE or similar systems; if historical data is sparse, algorithms will be trained on simulated or synthetic data.
Safety Services can autonomously trigger shutdown without being overridden by availability optimization logic; safety interlock is implemented and formally verified.

Evaluation period and measurement methodology are defined upfront (e.g., 'Availability is measured over 30-day rolling windows; unplanned downtime is any downtime not scheduled in advance; safety-driven shutdowns are excluded from unavailability penalty').
Site Manager and Maintenance team have access to Network Link and can receive real-time availability dashboard updates.
open_risks: RISK: Telemetry interface may not provide sufficient granularity for accurate predictive algorithms. MITIGATION: Conduct trade study on sensor types and sampling rates; define Telemetry data requirements upfront.
RISK: Failover latency may exceed safety requirements (e.g., >100ms). MITIGATION: Implement failover decision logic with hard real-time guarantees; conduct timing analysis and formal verification.
RISK: Redundancy cost may exceed customer budget. MITIGATION: Conduct cost-benefit analysis; consider phased implementation (MVP without redundancy, Phase 2 with redundancy).

RISK: Predictive maintenance algorithms may be unreliable due to sparse or non-representative training data. MITIGATION: Validate algorithms on historical data; implement fallback to reactive maintenance if predictions are unreliable.

RISK: Safety interlock may be inadvertently suppressed by availability optimization logic. MITIGATION: Implement explicit safety interlock; conduct formal verification of safety logic; use safety-critical design patterns (e.g., watchdog timers, dual-channel monitoring).

RISK: Dashboard integration with Site Manager's operational systems may be complex and time-consuming. MITIGATION: Define dashboard data format and API upfront; consider using standard protocols (e.g., REST, MQTT).
RISK: Availability measurement may be subjective (e.g., what counts as 'unplanned downtime'?). MITIGATION: Define measurement methodology upfront; conduct stakeholder alignment on definitions.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification (component-level health signals, sampling rate, data format, error handling).
Failover interface contract between NGHE Solution and Vehicle/Teleoperation (e.g., 'Vehicle.switchToRedundantMotor(timeout=100ms, callback=onFailoverComplete)').

Safety interlock protocol between NGHE Solution and Safety Services (e.g., 'SafetyAlert always takes precedence over availability optimization; NGHE Solution must acknowledge SafetyAlert within 10ms').
Predictive maintenance algorithm specification (e.g., remaining useful life estimation, anomaly detection, training data requirements).
Redundancy scope and cost-benefit analysis (which subsystems are critical enough to warrant redundancy? Does redundancy cost justify the availability improvement?).
Availability measurement methodology (evaluation period, unplanned downtime definition, safety-driven shutdown exclusion, statistical analysis approach).
Dashboard design and data format specification (uptime percentage, predicted failures, maintenance schedule, downtime root causes).
Historical failure data from NGHE or similar systems (for training predictive algorithms).
Formal verification of safety interlock logic (e.g., model checking, theorem proving).
Timing analysis and real-time verification of failover decision logic (e.g., worst-case latency <100ms).
